Probiotic TrialAnalysis Report
Overview1·Compare2·Right3·Wrong 4·Root Cause5·Trends6·Stats7·Insights8·Conclusions

Field Trial · L. vannamei · Dec 2025 – May 2026

Probiotic Pond Performance — Analysis Report

2J607 (OBT) was the standout pond — top biomass 5,437 kg (+4.7%), best FCR 1.67, highest survival 58.5% and +10% profit vs control. Yet the OBT group averaged below control (biomass −8.9%, survival −11.2%, FCR +9.4% worse): the probiotic effect was inconsistent, and survival — not growth or water quality — decided every outcome.

Control 2D407 vs OBT 2E707 · 2E807 · 2J607 · identical 505,000 PL stocking · 136–138 DOC · live NSA Final Analysis Sheet. Eight analysis sections, each point as its own card or interactive graph.

1 of 3 OBT beat control

The intelligence in one line

On average the three treated ponds trailed the control (biomass −8.9%, survival −11.2%, FCR +9.4% worse) — yet the single best pond, 2J607 (OBT), beat the control on every metric and was the only one more profitable (+10%). High variance within the treatment, not a clean win/loss. The master lever throughout was survival.

Section 1

Comparative Pond Analysis

Treated ponds vs the control across survival, biomass, ABW, growth, feed, FCR, productivity & harvest. All ponds stocked the same 505,000 PL, so these are pure outcome differences. Absolute and % differences shown.

1.1Harvest scorecard (absolute values, best→worst shaded)

Green = best, amber = middle, red = worst within each metric. FCR inverted (lower is better). Best value ringed.

1.2Head-to-head — absolute values

1.3% difference vs control

1.4How each pond scores on every metric

Each bar shows a pond as a % of the best pond on that metric (best = 100; FCR inverted so higher = better). The tallest bar wins — 2J607 leads almost everywhere.

1.5Productivity & feed consumed

Productivity = harvested biomass ÷ days of culture. Feed used is total feed delivered per pond.

Section 2

What Went Right

Positive outcomes, the evidence behind them, signs of a positive probiotic effect, and the business + technical implications.

2.1Best pond 2J607 — delta vs control

2J607 leads on biomass, survival & FCR while running the least aeration.

2.2Positive probiotic signal — growth (ABW)

OBT mean ABW 18.27 g vs control 17.71 g (+3.1%) — the one metric moving the same way across treated ponds.

Technical implication

2J607 shows the protocol's upside is real: best survival + above-average growth simultaneously, plus the lowest aeration load (11.97 HP vs 12.85 control) → ~$237 electricity saving. Replicating its dosing/aeration/alkalinity profile is the technical priority.

Business implication

2J607 returned +10% profit vs control ($14,697 vs $13,400) on near-identical cost. If reproducible across ponds, the gain scales directly with yield — but on current evidence (1 of 3) it is a promising lead, not a bankable result.

Section 3

What Went Wrong

Underperformers, water-quality issues, FCR inefficiency, survival/growth shortfalls, and the root cause of each — with supporting evidence.

3.1Water-quality issue — ammonia (TAN) anomaly

2J407 (sienna) runs far above all others — the signature of an overloaded pond. All other ponds stay near zero.

3.2FCR inefficiency tracks lost survival

Bars = FCR (left); line = survival (right). The two worst-survival ponds (2E707, 2E807) have the two worst FCRs.

Data note — pond 2J407: monitored 139 days but absent from the Harvest report & Financials, with the highest mean TAN (0.19 ppm) and nitrite (0.06 ppm) of any pond and the lowest morning DO (4.81 ppm). Treated as out-of-trial; please confirm its outcome.

Section 4

Root-Cause Analysis

What most influenced biomass, survival and FCR — and whether differences were driven by survival, growth, water quality, feed efficiency or management.

4.1What drove the biomass spread? (variability)

Coefficient of variation across the 4 ponds. Survival varied most (11.1%) > growth (7.0%) → survival drove biomass.

4.2Biomass decomposition: survivors × ABW

Bubble = harvested biomass. Right-and-up (more survivors, bigger shrimp) = bigger bubble. 2E807 is up-but-left (big shrimp, few animals).

4.3WQ ↔ performance correlations (n=4, directional)

Pearson r of pond-mean WQ vs outcome. Only directional clues — 4 ponds is far below significance.

4.4What most influenced each outcome

Biomass
Survival
CV 11.1% > ABW 7.0%; top-2 biomass = top-2 survival
Survival
Health / seed
WQ near-identical yet survival ranges 14.6 pts; not routine chemistry
FCR
Survival
Best FCR where survival best; dead shrimp waste feed
Verdict: performance differences were driven primarily by survival & pond-level health/management, with routine water chemistry minor and the probiotic adding at most a small, inconsistent growth effect. Feed efficiency was an outcome of survival, not an independent cause.

Section 5

Time-Series & Trend Analysis

When performance improved or declined, turning points and events, WQ trends and their effect on the shrimp, and the feed–growth relationship over the culture.

5.1Water-quality explorer (pick parameter, toggle ponds)

Parameter:Ponds:

5.2Growth curve with turning point

Curves track closely until ~DOC 90 (marker), then diverge as thinned ponds pull ahead on individual size.

5.3Feed ↔ growth relationship

Cumulative feed (area) vs ABW (line) for the selected pond. Feed delivery was near-identical across ponds.

5.4Turning points & events timeline

DOC 0 (25/27 Dec): stocking, 505,000 PL @ 0.1 g, ~20°C, salinity 40 ppt.
DOC 25–30: early plankton bloom — morning DO peaks ~6.5 ppm; healthiest WQ window.
DOC ~90 onward: morning DO begins sustained decline toward <4 ppm; growth curves diverge; rising biomass + seasonal warming (toward ~30°C) raise oxygen demand.
2J407 (timing varies): ammonia/nitrite climb above peers — overload signature.
DOC 136–138 (May): harvest. 2J607 & control retain highest survival; 2E807 thinned but large-shrimp.
WQ → performance effect: the late-culture dawn-DO squeeze is the clearest stress signal and coincides with peak biomass/feed load; pH, salinity and alkalinity stayed in comfort ranges and so did not constrain performance.

Section 6

Statistical Analysis

Mean, median and standard deviation; % change; correlations; variability and anomaly detection; and the confidence/limitations of each finding.

6.1Descriptive statistics — mean / median / SD

Parameter:

Whole-cycle daily values per pond. SD shows day-to-day variability within a pond.

6.2Between-pond variability & anomalies

CV of pond-mean WQ across ponds. TAN & nitrite vary most — driven by the 2J407 anomaly; core params (pH, salinity) barely move.

6.3Within-pond DO trend (robust, n≈136/pond)

Linear regression of morning DO on DOC. All ponds decline, |r|≈0.78–0.80, p«0.01 — the firmest statistical result.

6.4 Anomaly detection

  • 2J407 TAN 0.19 / NO₂ 0.06 ppm — 3–6× the trial mean; clear outlier.
  • 2E807 survival 43.9% — 14.6 pts below the best; standout low.
  • Late-culture morning DO <4 ppm in every pond — threshold breach.
  • 2E807 ABW 19.96 g — high outlier explained by low survival (thinning).

6.5 Confidence & significance

  • Treatment n=3, control n=1 — below replication for inferential testing; differences are descriptive.
  • Cross-pond correlations (n=4) are hypothesis-generating, often confounded — not proof.
  • Within-pond DO trend & the survival-drives-biomass decomposition are statistically/arithmetically robust.
  • No randomisation metadata — seed-batch & location effects can't be separated from treatment.

Section 7

Hidden Insights

Patterns easy to miss, trade-offs, delayed effects and early-warning indicators — each as its own card.

Pattern

Survival is the master variable

Survival varied more (CV 11.1%) than growth (7.0%). With identical stocking, ponds that protected survival (2J607, control) won on biomass, FCR and profit.

Trade-off

A high ABW can be a red flag

2E807's best-in-trial ABW came from heavy mortality thinning the pond — survivors had more food/space. Always read ABW next to survival / standing density.

Pattern

Profit hinges on kilograms, not cost

Total cost varied only ~2%; the entire ~$5,500 best-to-worst profit gap was a yield story. Focus on yield (survival), not input shaving.

Delayed effect

Probiotic's only consistent signal is growth

ABW moved the same way across treated ponds (+3.1%), hinting at a digestion/gut benefit that shows up late — but it did not protect survival.

Early warning

A delayed, system-wide oxygen squeeze

Morning DO declines gradually and identically in every pond — easy to miss daily, yet all breach 4 ppm at dawn after ~DOC 90. Trigger pre-emptive aeration then.

Early warning

2J407 is a case study in overload

Ammonia/nitrite climbing while DO runs lowest is the signature of an overloaded pond — an actionable trigger to cut feed and raise exchange before a crash.

Section 8

Report-Ready Conclusions

Each key conclusion as Finding → supporting data → interpretation → statistical support → recommendation.
